---
author: Tristan Muno
date: last-modified
date-format: MMMM D, YYYY
bibliography: ../references.bib
biblio-style: apsr
format:
  revealjs:
    embed-resources: true
    preview-links: false
    slideNumber: true
    incremental: true
    code-fold: show
    code-summary: "View source"
    pdfSeparateFragments: false
    footer: QMIR -- {{< meta date >}} -- Week 6
    logo: ./images/uma_ss.png
    theme: theme.scss
editor: source
execute:
  echo: true
  message: false
  warning: false
  freeze: true
---

## QMIR 2026

### Week 6: Probability, Simulation & Bayes' Theorem

![](./images/uma_palace.png){width="100%"} {{< meta author >}} <a href="https://github.com/dertristan" target="_blank" style="text-decoration: none; margin-left: 10px;"> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="20" height="20" fill="currentColor" class="bi bi-github" viewBox="0 0 16 16"> <path d="M8 0C3.58 0 0 3.58 0 8c0 3.54 2.29 6.53 5.47 7.59.4.07.55-.17.55-.38 0-.19-.01-.82-.01-1.49-2.01.37-2.53-.49-2.69-.94-.09-.23-.48-.94-.82-1.13-.28-.15-.68-.52-.01-.53.63-.01 1.08.58 1.23.82.72 1.21 1.87.87 2.33.66.07-.52.28-.87.51-1.07-1.78-.2-3.64-.89-3.64-3.95 0-.87.31-1.59.82-2.15-.08-.2-.36-1.02.08-2.12 0 0 .67-.21 2.2.82.64-.18 1.32-.27 2-.27s1.36.09 2 .27c1.53-1.04 2.2-.82 2.2-.82.44 1.1.16 1.92.08 2.12.51.56.82 1.27.82 2.15 0 3.07-1.87 3.75-3.65 3.95.29.25.54.73.54 1.48 0 1.07-.01 1.93-.01 2.2 0 .21.15.46.55.38A8.01 8.01 0 0 0 16 8c0-4.42-3.58-8-8-8"/> </svg> </a> <a href="mailto:tristan.muno@uni-mannheim.de" style="text-decoration: none; margin-left: 5px;"> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="20" height="20" fill="currentColor" class="bi bi-envelope" viewBox="0 0 16 16"> <path d="M0 4a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h12a2 2 0 0 1 2 2v8a2 2 0 0 1-2 2H2a2 2 0 0 1-2-2zm2-1a1 1 0 0 0-1 1v.217l7 4.2 7-4.2V4a1 1 0 0 0-1-1zm13 2.383-4.708 2.825L15 11.105zm-.034 6.876-5.64-3.471L8 9.583l-1.326-.795-5.64 3.47A1 1 0 0 0 2 13h12a1 1 0 0 0 .966-.741M1 11.105l4.708-2.897L1 5.383z"/> </svg> </a> <br> {{< meta date >}}

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
#| eval: true

library(tidyverse)

# ── Globe toss: 10-sided die parameterisation ─────────────────────────────────
# Faces: 0 = Land (L), 1 = Water (W)
# True proportion of water on Earth ≈ 0.7
# We model a 10-sided globe where each face is either L or W.
# Possible values of theta: 0/10, 1/10, 2/10, ..., 10/10

theta_discrete <- seq(0, 1, by = 0.1) # 11 hypotheses

# Uniform prior over all 11 faces
prior_uniform <- rep(1 / 11, 11)

# Example observation after class toss: 6 W in 9 tosses
W <- 6
N <- 9

# Likelihood: P(data | theta) under Binomial model
likelihood_discrete <- dbinom(W, size = N, prob = theta_discrete)

# Full Bayes theorem (with normalizing constant)
numerator_discrete <- likelihood_discrete * prior_uniform
marginal_likelihood <- sum(numerator_discrete) # P(data)
posterior_discrete <- numerator_discrete / marginal_likelihood

# ── Grid approximation: continuous theta ────────────────────────────────────
theta_grid <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 1000)
prior_flat <- rep(1, 1000)
likelihood_grid <- dbinom(W, size = N, prob = theta_grid)
unstd_posterior <- likelihood_grid * prior_flat
posterior_grid <- unstd_posterior / sum(unstd_posterior)

# ── Posteriors with different priors ────────────────────────────────────────
prior_skeptical <- ifelse(theta_grid < 0.5, 0, 1) # rules out < 50% water
prior_skeptical <- prior_skeptical / sum(prior_skeptical)

prior_informed <- dnorm(theta_grid, mean = 0.7, sd = 0.1)
prior_informed <- prior_informed / sum(prior_informed)

posterior_skeptical <- (likelihood_grid * prior_skeptical)
posterior_skeptical <- posterior_skeptical / sum(posterior_skeptical)

posterior_informed <- (likelihood_grid * prior_informed)
posterior_informed <- posterior_informed / sum(posterior_informed)

# ── More data ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
likelihood_big <- dbinom(60, size = 90, prob = theta_grid)
posterior_big <- likelihood_big * prior_flat
posterior_big <- posterior_big / sum(posterior_big)
```

## Week 6 Learning Goals {.smaller}

By the end of today, you should be able to:

1. Articulate the distinction between frequentist and Bayesian interpretations of probability
2. Explain why parameters are treated as uncertain quantities with distributions in the Bayesian framework
3. Name and interpret the three core ingredients of Bayesian inference: prior, likelihood, and posterior
4. State Bayes' theorem in full, identify each term, and explain the role of the normalizing constant $P(\text{data})$
5. Implement Bayesian updating by hand using a discrete 10-sided globe model
6. Apply grid approximation in R to compute and visualize a posterior distribution
7. Describe qualitatively how the posterior changes as data accumulates and as the prior changes

::: notes
These are the seven things students should be able to do walking out of the room. Notice the verbs: articulate, explain, name, state, implement, apply, describe. These are testable. The take-home exam will require several of these directly.

The session is deliberately structured so that items 1–4 are covered conceptually in the first half, and items 5–7 are operationalized in the coding segment. The homework deepens all seven.

Remind students at the start: today is a pivot. Block I was about tools. Block II is about reasoning. These are different cognitive demands. The programming will be simpler than weeks 3–5 -- the challenge today is conceptual.
:::

# Part I: The Opening Question

---

## The Globe Toss

::: {style="text-align: center; font-size: 1.4em; padding: 1em 0;"}
*[Placeholder: photograph or illustration of an inflatable globe ball being tossed in a lecture room]*
:::

::: {style="text-align: center;"}
**Catch the ball. Look at your right index finger. Call out: Land or Water.**
:::

::: notes
This is the live activity. Do NOT show slides during this segment. Stand at the front with the globe ball, toss it to students one by one, and record the results on the whiteboard as a simple W/L tally. Aim for 15–20 tosses. Keep the energy light and fast.

After the tosses, let the tally sit on the board and turn back to the room. Ask:

"Based on what we just observed -- what proportion of the Earth's surface is water?"

Take a few responses. Don't validate or correct. Then ask the follow-up sequence slowly:
- "How confident are you in that answer?"
- "Would your answer change if we only had 3 tosses? 300?"
- "What did you already believe *before* the first toss?"

That last question is the pivot. Students will realise they walked in with a prior -- they just never called it that. Let them feel that discomfort briefly before continuing.
:::

---

## What Did You Already Know?

::: {style="font-size: 0.8em"}
Before the first toss, you already believed:

- The proportion is probably not 5%
- The proportion is probably not 95%
- Values around 60–80% feel plausible
:::

. . .

That belief -- the one you held *before seeing any data* -- is a **prior**.

. . .

And then you updated it, toss by toss.


. . .

::: {.callout-note}
That process -- combining a prior belief with new evidence -- **is** Bayesian inference. Today we give it a formal mathematical structure.
:::

::: notes
The key move here is to make the implicit explicit. Students have been doing informal Bayesian reasoning all their lives. They just haven't named it.

The point about 5% and 95% being implausible is important: even a "flat" prior is not truly uninformative. We will return to this in the coding segment when we see that a uniform prior over [0, 1] is still a substantive choice -- it assigns equal probability to "0% water" and "70% water", which is arguably not what anyone really believes walking in.

Don't over-explain here. Let the idea breathe.
:::

---

## The Central Insight

::: {style="font-size: 1.3em; text-align: center;"}
**Everything uncertain gets a distribution.**

**The data is the one thing that is fixed.**
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.75em"}
In the Bayesian framework:

- The **parameter** $\theta$ -- the true proportion of water -- is uncertain. It gets a probability distribution.
- The **data** -- the actual tosses -- are observed facts. They are not uncertain. They happened.

::: {.callout-important}
This is an inversion of what many introductory courses imply. The parameter is *not* a fixed unknown constant that we estimate. It is a quantity about which we hold *graded beliefs*, and those beliefs are represented as a probability distribution.
:::

:::

::: notes
This is the single most important conceptual slide in the entire week. Take time here.

The standard frequentist framing -- "there is a true fixed parameter, and the data is random because it varies across hypothetical repeated samples" -- leads to statements like confidence intervals that are systematically misinterpreted. In Bayesian statistics, the inversion is principled: we condition on the data we actually observed, and we reason about our uncertainty over the parameter.

A good challenge question to pose to the room: "Is the proportion of water on Earth actually random?" No, of course not -- it's a physical fact. But *we are uncertain about it*, and probability is the language we use to express that uncertainty. The randomness is epistemic, not ontic. McElreath makes this point well in Statistical Rethinking ch. 1.

If a student pushes back ("but surely there IS a true value"), agree with them. Then ask: "And do you know it?" That's the point.
:::

# Part II: What Is Probability?

## Two Interpretations of Probability {.smaller}

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Frequentist**

Probability = long-run relative frequency across many repetitions of the same experiment.

$$P(A) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\text{count}(A)}{n}$$

- Probability is an objective property of a data-generating process
- Applies naturally to repeatable experiments (coin flips, dice)
- Does **not** apply to single events or to parameters

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Bayesian**

Probability = degree of rational belief, or plausibility, given available information.

$$P(A \mid \mathcal{I}) \in [0, 1]$$

- Probability is a statement about an agent's state of knowledge
- Applies to *any* uncertain proposition: events, parameters, hypotheses
- Updates coherently as new information arrives

:::
::::::

::: notes
Be careful not to strawman frequentism here. It is a coherent framework that has produced enormous scientific progress. The goal is not to dismiss it but to motivate why the Bayesian interpretation is more general and, for political science research, more naturally suited to the questions we ask.

The key limitation of frequentism for our purposes:
1. Many political science quantities are not the result of repeatable experiments. "What is the probability that the true effect of EU enlargement on GHG emissions is positive?" There is no long run of EU enlargements to average over.
2. Frequentist probability cannot be directly assigned to parameters -- parameters either have a specific value or they don't.
3. The frequentist confidence interval does NOT have the interpretation most people assign to it.

For the formal notation: the conditioning on I (information set) in the Bayesian formula is sometimes made explicit by writing P(A|I). This is a useful notational reminder that probability is always relative to what we know.

A useful line: "Frequentism asks: if I ran this experiment many times, how often would I observe this outcome? Bayesianism asks: given what I've observed, what should I believe? The second question is usually the one we actually care about."
:::

---

## Why This Matters for Political Science

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.65em"}
**Questions we cannot answer with frequentist probability:**

- "What is the probability that the treatment effect is positive?"
- "How likely is it that Country X will democratize in the next decade?"
- "Given the data, what should we believe about the effect of GDP on emissions?"
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.65em"}
**Why frequentist probability struggles here:**

- There is no long run of repetitions to average over
- Parameters do not have frequency distributions -- they are fixed (but unknown) constants
- The confidence interval is systematically misinterpreted, even by researchers
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.65em"}
::: {.callout-note}
A 95% confidence interval does **not** mean "there is a 95% probability the true parameter lies in this interval." It means: if we repeated this sampling procedure many times, 95% of the resulting intervals would contain the true value. The true value either is or is not in *this particular* interval -- we just don't know which.
:::
:::

::: notes
The confidence interval misinterpretation is one of the most persistent errors in applied statistics. Hoekstra et al. (2014, Psychonomic Bulletin & Review) found that the majority of researchers, including statisticians, misinterpret confidence intervals. The Bayesian credible interval, by contrast, directly supports the intuitive interpretation: "there is a 90% probability that theta lies in this interval, given the data and prior."

This is a good moment to ask students: "How many of you have seen a paper that says 'the 95% CI ranges from X to Y, suggesting the true effect lies in this range'?" Almost everyone will say yes. That IS the misinterpretation. It is ubiquitous.

Do not dwell too long here -- the goal is to motivate, not to fully adjudicate the frequentist-Bayesian debate. The pragmatism point from the syllabus applies: Bayesian tools serve our research questions better. That is the argument.
:::

# Part III: The Three Ingredients

## The Three Ingredients of Bayesian Inference

::: {style="font-size: 1.1em; text-align: center; padding: 0.5em 0;"}

$$
\underbrace{P(\theta \mid \text{data})}_{\text{Posterior}} = \frac{\underbrace{P(\text{data} \mid \theta)}_{\text{Likelihood}} \cdot \underbrace{P(\theta)}_{\text{Prior}}}{\underbrace{P(\text{data})}_{\text{Marginal Likelihood}}}
$$

:::

::: notes
This is the first time students see the full formula with the denominator. Present it in this order: name the ingredients before writing the formula, so that when the formula appears, each symbol has a meaning already attached to it.

The normalizing constant P(data) is the marginal likelihood -- it is the probability of observing the data we observed, averaged over all possible values of theta, weighted by the prior:

P(data) = integral over theta of P(data|theta) * P(theta) d(theta)

This is a scalar -- a single number, not a function of theta. It does not affect the *shape* of the posterior, only its scale, ensuring the posterior integrates to 1. This is why we can often write the proportional form. BUT the full version with the denominator is the correct starting point. In week 7 we will see precisely why computing P(data) is often intractable, which is what motivates MCMC.

Important: the likelihood P(data|theta) is NOT a probability distribution over theta. It is a function of theta that gives the probability of the observed data under each possible parameter value. This is a common point of confusion.
:::

---

## The Three Ingredients of Bayesian Inference

::: {style="font-size: 0.5em; text-align: center; padding: 0.2em 0;"}

$$
\underbrace{P(\theta \mid \text{data})}_{\text{Posterior}} = \frac{\underbrace{P(\text{data} \mid \theta)}_{\text{Likelihood}} \cdot \underbrace{P(\theta)}_{\text{Prior}}}{\underbrace{P(\text{data})}_{\text{Marginal Likelihood}}}
$$

:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.65em"}

| Term | Symbol | Plain language |
|---|---|--------------------|
| **Prior** | $P(\theta)$ | What we believe about $\theta$ *before* seeing data |
| **Likelihood** | $P(\text{data} \mid \theta)$ | How probable is the data, *given* a value of $\theta$? |
| **Marginal Likelihood** | $P(\text{data})$ | Overall probability of the data under the prior |
| **Posterior** | $P(\theta \mid \text{data})$ | Updated belief about $\theta$ *after* seeing data |

:::

---

## Ingredient 1: The Prior $P(\theta)$ {.smaller}

**The prior encodes what we believe about $\theta$ before observing data.**

. . .

For the globe toss, $\theta \in [0, 1]$ is the proportion of the Earth's surface covered by water.

. . .

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Flat (uninformative) prior:**  
Every value of $\theta$ is equally plausible.  
$$
P(\theta) = \text{Uniform}(0, 1)
$$

**Weakly informative prior:**  
We know the Earth is not mostly desert.  
$$
P(\theta) = \text{Normal}(0.7, 0.2) \text{ [truncated to } [0,1]]
$$

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Skeptical prior:**  
We believe the surface is unlikely to be less than half water.

**Informative prior:**  
Previous scientific measurements constrain our beliefs strongly.

:::
::::::

. . .

::: {.callout-important}
**Priors are assumptions -- not bias.** Every analysis makes assumptions. Bayesian analysis makes them *explicit and auditable*. A flat prior is still a prior -- it asserts, for example, that "0% water" is as plausible as "70% water", which is arguably not what anyone believes.
:::

::: notes
This is often where students (and critics) object: "isn't it subjective to choose a prior?" The response has several layers:

1. Every analysis makes prior assumptions -- about functional form, inclusion of variables, significance thresholds. Bayesian analysis simply makes those assumptions visible.

2. A flat prior is not "no assumption" -- it is the assumption that all values are equally plausible. For many scientific questions, this is actually the most extreme possible assumption.

3. The prior's influence diminishes as data accumulates (we will show this in the coding segment). With large samples, two analysts who started with very different priors will converge to nearly identical posteriors.

4. Sensitivity analysis: in good Bayesian practice, you examine how much your conclusions depend on your prior. If they change dramatically with small changes to the prior, that is scientifically informative -- your data is not very informative about your question.

5. Choosing a significance level of alpha = 0.05 in frequentist analysis is also a subjective choice. It is arguably more arbitrary than a theoretically motivated prior, because it has no connection to the specific scientific question.

Reference: Gelman & Shalizi (2013, British Journal of Mathematical and Statistical Psychology) for a thoughtful defence of Bayesian prior choice.
:::

---

## Ingredient 2: The Likelihood $P(\text{data} \mid \theta)$

::: {style="font-size: 0.65em"}
**The likelihood scores each possible value of $\theta$ by how well it explains the data.**
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.65em"}
For the globe toss, our data-generating model is the **Binomial distribution**:

$$P(\text{data} \mid \theta) = P(W, N \mid \theta) = \binom{N}{W} \theta^W (1 - \theta)^{N - W}$$

where $W$ = number of water observations, $N$ = total tosses.

- If $\theta = 0.7$: tossing the globe 9 times and getting 6 waters is relatively probable
- If $\theta = 0.1$: getting 6 waters in 9 tosses is very improbable
- The likelihood **is not a probability distribution over $\theta$** -- it does not integrate to 1 over $\theta$
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.65em"}
::: {.callout-note}
The likelihood is a function of $\theta$ that tells us which parameter values are *consistent* with the observed data. It is the formal bridge between the model and the observations.
:::
:::

::: notes
Emphasize the distinction: the Binomial distribution is written as a function of W given theta, N. When we fix W and N (our observations) and vary theta, we get the likelihood *function*. This is the same mathematical expression, but we are holding the data fixed and treating theta as the variable.

The maximum of the likelihood function -- the value of theta that makes the data most probable -- is the Maximum Likelihood Estimate (MLE). In this case, MLE = W/N = 6/9 ≈ 0.67. Students who have seen frequentist regression know MLE -- this connects the frameworks.

The fact that the likelihood does not integrate to 1 over theta is important and often confused. P(W=6, N=9 | theta=0.7) is a perfectly well-defined probability. But integrating this over all theta from 0 to 1 gives a number that is generally not 1. This is why the normalizing constant P(data) in the denominator of Bayes' theorem is needed -- it rescales the numerator so that the posterior *does* integrate to 1.
:::

---

## Ingredient 3: The Posterior $P(\theta \mid \text{data})$

::: {style="font-size: 0.65em"}
**The posterior is the distribution of $\theta$ after observing data. It is the answer.**
:::
. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.65em"}
$$
P(\theta \mid \text{data}) = \frac{P(\text{data} \mid \theta) \cdot P(\theta)}{P(\text{data})}
$$
:::
. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.65em"}
- The posterior is always a **full probability distribution** over $\theta$ -- not a point estimate, not a $p$-value
- It expresses our *remaining uncertainty* after combining prior knowledge and evidence
- It is more concentrated than the prior -- data reduces uncertainty
- **Today's posterior is tomorrow's prior** -- Bayesian updating is sequential and coherent
:::
. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.5em"}
::: {.callout-tip}
**The posterior contains everything we know about $\theta$ after observing the data.** Point summaries (mean, median, mode) can be computed from it, but the full distribution is richer. In later weeks, we will routinely work with the full posterior rather than just its summary statistics.
:::
:::

::: notes
Key points to hammer home:
1. "Full distribution" -- this is the rhetorical payoff of everything so far. After all this work, we don't get a number, we get a distribution. That feels like more work for less output. But it is actually more: it carries full uncertainty quantification. In contrast, a frequentist point estimate discards all the information about uncertainty, retaining only the single most likely value.

2. "Sequential coherence" -- this is one of the most powerful properties of Bayesian inference. If we see new data, we don't have to start over. We just use the current posterior as the new prior. This is exactly what rational belief updating looks like.

3. The mode of the posterior (MAP estimate -- Maximum A Posteriori) corresponds to the MLE when the prior is flat. This connection is useful for students who already know regression: OLS regression is a special case of MAP estimation with a flat prior and Gaussian likelihood.
:::

# Part IV: The Normalizing Constant

## The Role of $P(\text{data})$

::: {style="font-size: 0.65em"}
$$
P(\theta \mid \text{data}) = \frac{P(\text{data} \mid \theta) \cdot P(\theta)}{P(\text{data})}
$$
:::
. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.55em"}
**What is $P(\text{data})$?**

The *marginal likelihood* -- the overall probability of the observed data, averaged over all possible parameter values:

$$
P(\text{data}) = \int_{\Theta} P(\text{data} \mid \theta) \cdot P(\theta) \, d\theta
$$

In the discrete case:

$$P(\text{data}) = \sum_{\theta \in \Theta} P(\text{data} \mid \theta) \cdot P(\theta)$$
:::
. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.55em"}
**What does it do?**

It ensures the posterior is a proper probability distribution: $\int P(\theta \mid \text{data}) \, d\theta = 1$.
:::
. . .

::: {.callout-important style="font-size: 0.65em"}
$P(\text{data})$ does **not** depend on $\theta$. It is a normalizing constant -- the same number for every value of $\theta$. This is why, in practice, we often write: $P(\theta \mid \text{data}) \propto P(\text{data} \mid \theta) \cdot P(\theta)$.

But the full equation is always the correct starting point.
:::

::: notes
This slide is important because it sets up the cliffhanger for week 7. The proportional form (with ∝ instead of =) is convenient and widely used. But it only works because P(data) is a constant with respect to theta. When we need the full posterior (not just its shape), we need P(data).

The key insight for week 7: computing the integral in the denominator is often analytically intractable. For simple models with conjugate priors (like Beta-Binomial), it has a closed form. But for most realistic statistical models -- multivariate regression, multilevel models -- this integral has no closed-form solution. This is the computational problem that MCMC solves.

For the discrete globe example: P(data) = sum over all 11 hypotheses of P(data|theta) * P(theta). Students will compute this explicitly in the coding segment. This makes the abstract formula completely concrete.

Note for the mathematically inclined: the full Bayesian theorem with the denominator is sometimes called the "Bayes-Price theorem" or attributed to Laplace. The proportional form is a convenience, not the fundamental theorem.
:::

---

## Why the Denominator Is Hard {.smaller}

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="55%"}

**For the globe toss with 10 faces:** the sum is easy.

$$P(\text{data}) = \sum_{k=0}^{10} P(W, N \mid \theta_k) \cdot P(\theta_k)$$

We sum over 11 values -- trivial.

**For a regression model with 2 parameters:**

$$P(\text{data}) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} P(\text{data} \mid \alpha, \beta) \cdot P(\alpha, \beta) \, d\alpha \, d\beta$$

A two-dimensional integral -- manageable.

:::

::: {.column width="45%"}

**For a multilevel model with $k$ parameters:**

$$P(\text{data}) = \int_{\mathbb{R}^k} P(\text{data} \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}) \cdot P(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \, d\boldsymbol{\theta}$$

A $k$-dimensional integral. **Often has no analytical solution.**

::: {.callout-note}
This is precisely the problem that **MCMC** (next week) solves -- not by computing $P(\text{data})$, but by sampling from the posterior *without needing to evaluate the denominator at all*.
:::

:::
::::::

::: notes
This slide plants the seed for week 7. The message is: the full Bayes theorem is correct and complete, but computing it for realistic models is computationally intractable because of the integral in the denominator.

MCMC's genius is that it sidesteps the denominator entirely. It constructs a Markov chain whose stationary distribution IS the posterior, without ever computing the normalizing constant. This is why the proportional form P(theta|data) ∝ P(data|theta) * P(theta) is so central to MCMC algorithms -- the algorithms only ever evaluate the numerator.

For the discrete 10-sided globe: the denominator is a sum over 11 terms, computable by hand. Students will do this in the exercise. This makes the abstract concrete before we reveal that the same logic becomes infeasible at scale.
:::

# Part V: The 10-Sided Globe

## A Tractable Model: The 10-Sided Globe {.smaller}

::: {style="font-size: 0.9em;"}

Imagine the Earth as a **10-sided polyhedron**. Each face is either Land (L) or Water (W).

The unknown parameter $\theta$ = proportion of water faces.

$$
\theta \in \left\{\frac{0}{10}, \frac{1}{10}, \frac{2}{10}, \ldots, \frac{10}{10}\right\}
$$

There are exactly **11 hypotheses** -- one for each possible number of water faces.

:::

. . .

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Prior:** We start with no preference -- every hypothesis is equally plausible.

$$P(\theta_k) = \frac{1}{11} \quad \text{for all } k \in \{0, 1, \ldots, 10\}$$

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Observed data:** In 9 tosses, we observe $W = 6$ waters.

$$\text{data} = (W = 6, N = 9)$$

:::
::::::

---

## A Tractable Model: The 10-Sided Globe {.smaller}

::: {.callout-note}
The 10-sided model is a **simplification** -- the real globe has a continuous parameter space. But the discrete version makes every step of Bayes' theorem computable by hand, and the logic is identical. Once you understand it here, the continuous case follows directly.
:::

::: notes
This is the McElreath-style pedagogical move: simplify to the point of tractability without losing the logic. A 10-sided globe means we have 11 explicit hypotheses that we can write down, score, and update one at a time. There is no calculus, no numerical integration -- just arithmetic.

The fact that the prior assigns 1/11 to each hypothesis is the discrete version of a uniform prior. It is a strong assumption -- it says we think 0% water is as plausible as 70% water. This is worth flagging to students as a limitation, even while using it for pedagogical convenience.

The connection to the continuous case: as the number of faces increases to infinity, the discrete distribution over theta converges to a continuous prior, and the discrete sum in the denominator converges to an integral. The 10-sided globe is the finite approximation.
:::

---

## Computing the Likelihood {.smaller}

For each hypothesis $\theta_k = k/10$, compute how probable the observed data $(W=6, N=9)$ would be:

$$P(W = 6, N = 9 \mid \theta_k) = \binom{9}{6} \theta_k^6 (1 - \theta_k)^3$$

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.65em;"}

| $k$ | $\theta_k$ | $P(W{=}6, N{=}9 \mid \theta_k)$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0.0 | 0.000 |
| 1 | 0.1 | 0.000 |
| 2 | 0.2 | 0.003 |
| 3 | 0.3 | 0.021 |
| 4 | 0.4 | 0.074 |
| 5 | 0.5 | 0.164 |
| 6 | 0.6 | 0.251 |
| ***7*** | ***0.7*** | ***0.267*** |
| 8 | 0.8 | 0.176 |
| 9 | 0.9 | 0.045 |
| 10 | 1.0 | 0.000 |

:::

. . .

The likelihood **peaks at $\theta = 0.7$** -- exactly $W/N = 6/9$. This is the MLE.

::: notes
Walk through this table carefully. The binomial coefficient C(9,6) = 84 is the same for every row -- it counts the number of ways to arrange 6 waters in 9 tosses. What changes row by row is theta^6 * (1-theta)^3.

At theta=0.0: any water observation is impossible, so likelihood = 0.
At theta=1.0: any land observation is impossible, so likelihood = 0.
The peak at theta=0.7 makes intuitive sense: if you believed 70% of the faces were water, you would predict a 6/9 outcome more strongly than any other hypothesis.

This is also a good moment to connect to OLS/MLE: the maximum likelihood estimate is 6/9 = 0.667, approximately 0.7 in our discrete grid. In frequentist maximum likelihood, this would be our estimate. In Bayesian analysis, we use the full likelihood function -- not just its maximum -- to update the prior.

The 0.000 entries are not exactly zero in general (except for theta=0 and theta=1 in this case). Students might ask about this -- it is worth confirming: dbinom(6, 9, 0) = 0 exactly because 6 waters is impossible if theta=0.
:::

---

## Computing the Full Posterior {.smaller}

$$
P(\theta_k \mid \text{data}) = \frac{P(\text{data} \mid \theta_k) \cdot P(\theta_k)}{P(\text{data})} = \frac{P(\text{data} \mid \theta_k) \cdot \frac{1}{11}}{\sum_{j=0}^{10} P(\text{data} \mid \theta_j) \cdot \frac{1}{11}}
$$

. . .

Since $P(\theta_k) = 1/11$ is the same for all $k$, the $1/11$ cancels:

$$P
(\theta_k \mid \text{data}) = \frac{P(\text{data} \mid \theta_k)}{\sum_{j=0}^{10} P(\text{data} \mid \theta_j)}
$$

. . .

The normalizing constant is simply:

$$
P(\text{data}) = \sum_{j=0}^{10} P(\text{data} \mid \theta_j) \cdot \frac{1}{11} = \frac{1}{11} \sum_{j=0}^{10} P(\text{data} \mid \theta_j) \approx \frac{1}{11} \times 1.001 \approx 0.091
$$

::: notes
This is the payoff of using a discrete model: students can literally see the sum in the denominator being computed. The sum of all the likelihood values in the previous table (scaled by 1/11) gives the marginal likelihood.

The exact value: sum of the likelihoods = 0.000 + 0.000 + 0.003 + 0.021 + 0.074 + 0.164 + 0.251 + 0.267 + 0.176 + 0.045 + 0.000 = 1.001 (slight rounding). Divided by 11 gives approximately 0.091.

Then each posterior value is: likelihood_k / sum(likelihoods).

Key insight to deliver here: with a uniform prior, the posterior is just the normalized likelihood. The prior is doing nothing in terms of shape -- only the data matters. This will change dramatically when we use an informative prior in the coding exercise.
:::

---

## The Posterior Distribution

```{r}
#| label: fig-discrete-posterior
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 4
#| fig-cap: "Prior (flat), likelihood, and posterior for the 10-sided globe model. Data: W=6, N=9."

tibble(
  theta = theta_discrete,
  prior = prior_uniform,
  likelihood = likelihood_discrete / sum(likelihood_discrete),
  posterior = posterior_discrete
) |>
  pivot_longer(-theta, names_to = "distribution", values_to = "probability") |>
  mutate(
    distribution = factor(
      distribution,
      levels = c("prior", "likelihood", "posterior"),
      labels = c("Prior P(θ)", "Likelihood (normalised)", "Posterior P(θ|data)")
    )
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = theta, y = probability, fill = distribution)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.08) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = theta_discrete,
    labels = paste0(seq(0, 10), "/10")
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("steelblue", "goldenrod3", "firebrick"),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom", aspect.ratio = 0.5) +
  labs(
    x = "θ (proportion of water)",
    y = "Probability",
    caption = "Flat prior; Binomial likelihood; W = 6, N = 9"
  )
```

Under flat (uninformative) priors, the Posterior takes the form of the likelihood.

::: notes
This plot shows the three ingredients side by side for all 11 hypotheses. With a flat prior, the posterior is simply the normalized likelihood -- the shape is driven entirely by the data.

Key observations to point out:
- The prior is flat -- every bar has the same height (1/11).
- The likelihood peaks at theta = 7/10 = 0.7.
- The posterior therefore also peaks at 0.7.
- Hypotheses with very low likelihood (0/10, 1/10, 9/10, 10/10) get very low posterior weight.

Ask students: "What would happen if we had a prior that concentrated probability on the left side (small theta values)?" The posterior would shift left relative to the likelihood. This is exactly what they will explore in the exercise.
:::

---

## Updating Sequentially

The Bayesian machine updates one observation at a time. **Today's posterior is tomorrow's prior.**

. . .

::: {style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;"}

*[Placeholder: animated or multi-panel figure showing posterior updating after 1, 3, 6, 9 tosses from left to right, each starting from the previous posterior]*

:::

. . .

**After 0 tosses:** Posterior = Prior (flat)

**After 1 toss (W):** Posterior shifts slightly toward higher $\theta$

**After 3 tosses (WWL):** Moderate concentration around $\theta \approx 0.6$

**After 9 tosses (WWLWWWLWW):** Posterior concentrates sharply around $\theta \approx 0.7$

. . .

::: {.callout-note}
This sequential updating property is a formal expression of the scientific ideal: revise beliefs in proportion to evidence. It is also computationally useful: we do not need all data at once. We can update the model as data arrives, and the result is always the same as fitting the full dataset from scratch.
:::

::: notes
The sequential updating property has deep implications:
1. It formalizes the scientific method as coherent belief revision.
2. It makes Bayesian models naturally suited to streaming or longitudinal data.
3. It can be shown that the result of sequential updating is identical to the result of fitting the model to all data simultaneously (for exchangeable observations). This is an important theoretical result.

If time allows, you can demonstrate this in R: compute the posterior after 3 tosses, use it as the prior for the next 6 tosses, and show the result is identical to the posterior computed from all 9 tosses at once.

The placeholder for an animation is intentional -- this is a good candidate for an actual animation (using gganimate or a sequence of ggplots) if you want to add it before the session.
:::

# Part VI: Grid Approximation in R

---

## From 10 Faces to 1000 Grid Points

The logic is identical -- we just make $\theta$ denser.

```{r}
#| label: grid-approx-code
#| echo: true
#| eval: false

# 1. Define the grid
theta_grid <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 1000)

# 2. Prior (flat)
prior <- rep(1, 1000)

# 3. Likelihood: P(data | theta) for W=6, N=9
likelihood <- dbinom(6, size = 9, prob = theta_grid)

# 4. Numerator: likelihood × prior
numerator <- likelihood * prior

# 5. Normalizing constant: P(data) = sum of numerator
marginal_likelihood <- sum(numerator)

# 6. Posterior: divide by P(data)
posterior <- numerator / marginal_likelihood
```

. . .

::: {.callout-note}
We divide by `sum(numerator)` -- the discrete approximation of $\int P(\text{data} \mid \theta) P(\theta) \, d\theta$. This **is** $P(\text{data})$. We are computing Bayes' theorem in full, not just using the proportional shortcut.
:::

::: notes
Walk through this code line by line. Every line corresponds to a term in Bayes' theorem:
- Line 2: the prior P(theta)
- Line 3: the likelihood P(data|theta)
- Line 4: the numerator P(data|theta) * P(theta)
- Line 5: P(data) -- the marginal likelihood, the normalizing constant
- Line 6: the full posterior P(theta|data) = numerator / P(data)

The connection to the 10-sided globe is direct: instead of 11 values of theta, we have 1000. The arithmetic is the same -- we just do it for more hypotheses.

Emphasize that `sum(numerator)` is the discrete approximation of the integral in the denominator. On a fine enough grid (1000 points), this is a very accurate approximation.

After running this code, the posterior should sum to 1: `sum(posterior)` = 1 (up to floating point precision). This is a good sanity check students can run.
:::

---

## The Three Curves

```{r}
#| label: fig-grid-three-curves
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 4
#| fig-cap: "Prior, likelihood, and posterior over a 1000-point grid. Flat prior; W = 6, N = 9."

tibble(
  theta = theta_grid,
  Prior = prior_flat / sum(prior_flat),
  Likelihood = likelihood_grid / sum(likelihood_grid),
  Posterior = posterior_grid
) |>
  pivot_longer(-theta, names_to = "distribution", values_to = "density") |>
  mutate(
    distribution = factor(
      distribution,
      levels = c("Prior", "Likelihood", "Posterior")
    )
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = theta, y = density, color = distribution)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("steelblue", "goldenrod3", "firebrick"),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom", aspect.ratio = 0.5) +
  labs(
    x = "θ (proportion of water)",
    y = "Density (normalised)",
    caption = "Flat prior; Binomial likelihood; W = 6, N = 9"
  )
```

. . .

With a flat prior, the **posterior = normalised likelihood**. The prior contributes no information -- only the data shapes the posterior.

::: notes
Key observation: with a flat prior, the posterior is just the normalized likelihood. The prior has no influence on the shape. This is not always desirable -- flat priors can assign non-trivial prior mass to implausible regions of parameter space, and they can be "improper" (not integrating to 1) in some parameterizations.

The posterior peaks at theta = 6/9 = 0.667, close to 0.7 on our grid. The spread reflects our remaining uncertainty after 9 observations.

Notice the x-axis is continuous now rather than discrete. The transition from the 10-sided globe to the continuous grid should feel natural -- it is the same logic at higher resolution.
:::

---

## What Happens With More Data?

```{r}
#| label: fig-more-data
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 4
#| fig-cap: "Posterior after 9 tosses (W=6) vs. 90 tosses (W=60). Flat prior."

tibble(
  theta = theta_grid,
  `9 tosses (W=6)` = posterior_grid,
  `90 tosses (W=60)` = posterior_big
) |>
  pivot_longer(-theta, names_to = "n_tosses", values_to = "posterior") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = theta, y = posterior, color = n_tosses)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("steelblue", "firebrick"), name = NULL) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom", aspect.ratio = 0.5) +
  labs(
    x = "θ (proportion of water)",
    y = "Posterior density",
    caption = "Flat prior; proportion of water held constant at W/N = 2/3"
  )
```

. . .

More data → **narrower posterior** → **less remaining uncertainty**. The *location* (where the peak is) barely moves; the *width* (our uncertainty) shrinks substantially.

::: notes
This is one of the most important empirical properties of Bayesian posteriors: as data accumulates, the posterior concentrates. The location of the posterior converges to the true parameter value (Bayesian consistency), and the width shrinks at rate 1/sqrt(N).

Two things to point out:
1. The peak barely moves -- the data proportion is held constant at 2/3 in both cases, so the MLE is the same. The posterior is just more certain about it.
2. The width shrinks dramatically. This is the visual representation of "more data = less uncertainty."

Ask students: "What would happen if we had 900 tosses? 9000?" The posterior would become almost a spike at the true value. In the limit of infinite data, all reasonable priors lead to the same posterior -- the data overwhelms the prior.
:::

---

## What Happens With a Different Prior?

```{r}
#| label: fig-different-priors
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 4.2
#| fig-cap: "Posterior under three different priors. W = 6, N = 9."

tibble(
  theta = theta_grid,
  `Flat prior` = posterior_grid,
  `Skeptical prior\n(θ ≥ 0.5 only)` = posterior_skeptical,
  `Informed prior\nN(0.7, 0.1)` = posterior_informed
) |>
  pivot_longer(-theta, names_to = "prior_type", values_to = "posterior") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = theta, y = posterior, color = prior_type)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("steelblue", "goldenrod3", "firebrick"),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom", aspect.ratio = 0.5) +
  labs(
    x = "θ (proportion of water)",
    y = "Posterior density",
    caption = "W = 6, N = 9; same data, different priors"
  )
```

. . .

With only 9 tosses, the prior matters. With 90 tosses, all three posteriors would nearly coincide.

::: notes
Three priors are shown:
1. Flat: uniform over [0,1]. Posterior = normalized likelihood.
2. Skeptical: zero mass below 0.5 (we rule out "mostly land" worlds). The posterior is truncated and shifted right.
3. Informed: concentrated near 0.7 with sd=0.1, representing prior scientific knowledge. The posterior is pulled toward 0.7 and slightly narrower.

The key lesson: with 9 observations, the prior still has substantial influence. The informed prior and the data agree here (both point to ~0.7), so they reinforce each other. The skeptical prior is also compatible with the data (it only rules out theta < 0.5, which is already low-probability territory).

Ask: "What if we used an informed prior centered at 0.3?" The posterior would be a compromise between prior and data. With 9 tosses, the prior could still pull the posterior meaningfully away from the MLE.

Self-correcting property: as N → ∞, all three posteriors converge to the same value regardless of prior.
:::

# Part VII: Summary & Bridge to Week 7

---

## Three Ideas to Take Away

. . .

**1. Probability is a language for uncertainty, not just frequency.**

Probability quantifies degrees of rational belief. This makes it applicable to parameters, hypotheses, and any proposition about which we are uncertain.

. . .

**2. Everything uncertain gets a distribution.**

Parameters are uncertain; we represent that uncertainty with probability distributions. The data is fixed -- we observed it. The parameter is what we infer.

. . .

**3. The posterior is prior × likelihood, normalized.**

$$P(\theta \mid \text{data}) = \frac{P(\text{data} \mid \theta) \cdot P(\theta)}{P(\text{data})}$$

The posterior carries full uncertainty. It is richer than a point estimate. It is the starting point for all summaries, predictions, and decisions.

::: notes
These are the three ideas that should be retained from today. If a student can explain each of these to a non-statistician friend, they have understood the session.

Reinforce the connection to research practice:
- In weeks 8–13, every model we fit will produce a posterior distribution over regression coefficients, intercepts, variance parameters, etc.
- We will always ask: what do we believe about this parameter? How much uncertainty do we have? The posterior answers both.
- The prior forces us to be explicit about assumptions -- this is epistemically humble, not subjective.

Take any remaining questions before moving to the cliffhanger.
:::

---

## The Cliffhanger: Why Grid Approximation Is Not Enough

Consider a simple linear regression model with parameters $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\sigma$.

. . .

A grid of 1000 points per parameter requires:

$$1000^3 = 1{,}000{,}000{,}000 \text{ grid cells}$$

. . .

A multilevel model with 50 parameters:

$$1000^{50} \approx 10^{150} \text{ grid cells}$$

*The observable universe contains approximately $10^{80}$ atoms.*

. . .

::: {.callout-important}
**The curse of dimensionality makes grid approximation infeasible for any realistic model.** We need a fundamentally different approach -- one that *explores* the posterior rather than *evaluating* it everywhere.

That approach is **Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC)**. And the way to understand it involves a king, a chain of islands, and a surprisingly simple rule about when to move and when to stay.
:::

::: notes
The cursor of dimensionality is a general phenomenon in high-dimensional spaces. In low dimensions, a grid is fine. In higher dimensions, the volume of parameter space explodes exponentially, and the vast majority of that space has near-zero posterior density. A grid wastes almost all its computation in irrelevant regions.

MCMC's genius: it doesn't evaluate the posterior everywhere. It constructs a random walk that, over time, visits regions of parameter space in proportion to their posterior probability. It spends its time where the posterior mass actually is, not where it isn't.

The island king (Metropolis algorithm) is the cleanest intuitive entry point to this idea. The rule: propose a move; if the proposed location has higher posterior, move; if lower, move with probability proportional to the ratio. The emergent behavior is that the chain visits each location proportional to its posterior probability.

End on this cliffhanger. Do NOT resolve it -- let the curiosity carry students into week 7.
:::

---

## Preview: Week 7

. . .

- The **Metropolis algorithm**: a random walk that recovers the posterior
- **What a chain is** and what the samples it produces *represent*
- **Trace plots**, burn-in, convergence, $\hat{R}$, effective sample size -- conceptual understanding, not diagnostic recipes
- **brms as a black box** -- the king's journey made practical

. . .

::: {style="text-align: center; padding: 1em; font-size: 0.95em;"}
*After next week, the engine will no longer be a black box.*
:::

::: notes
Brief preview only -- don't explain MCMC today. The cliffhanger should create genuine curiosity.

One sentence to leave them with: "After next week, when brms prints 'Chains: 4, Iterations: 2000', you will know exactly what that means and why it is there."
:::

# Time to Practice!

---

## Exercise Overview

- Clone the Week 6 exercise repo from GitHub
- Open `exercise_week06.qmd` in Positron
- You will implement the 10-sided globe model and grid approximation from scratch
- Tasks follow the arc of today's session: **discrete → continuous → sensitivity analysis**
- **Fast finishers:** optional extension on posterior summaries

::: notes
The exercise is structured in three parts matching the session:
1. Discrete 10-sided globe: compute likelihood, posterior, and normalizing constant by hand (in R), then plot.
2. Grid approximation: implement the full pipeline for the continuous case.
3. Prior sensitivity: try three different priors, compare posteriors, write interpretive sentences.

Optional extension: posterior sampling and credible intervals -- covered in the homework.

Reserve the last 10 minutes for a homework preview. Show students what a 90% credible interval looks like and ask them to contrast it verbally with a 90% confidence interval. This plants the seed for the main homework question.
:::

---

## Homework {.smaller}

- Distributed via GitHub Classroom (same workflow as previous weeks)
- Submit a **rendered Quarto document** (`.qmd` + rendered PDF)
- **Due:** one week from today (Mon → next Mon 13:30; Thu → next Thu 18:45)

. . .

**What you will produce:**

1. **Core:** Re-implement the 10-sided globe model using the actual class toss data. Plot prior, likelihood, and posterior. Compute $P(\text{data})$ explicitly and report it.

2. **Core:** Grid approximation with the class data. Try three different priors. For each, write 2–3 sentences: what belief does it encode, and is it scientifically defensible for the globe toss?

3. **Stretch:** Draw 10,000 samples from the grid posterior. Compute the posterior mean, median, and 90% credible interval. Plot as histogram + density overlay.

4. **Stretch:** Write one sentence interpreting the credible interval in plain language. Then write a paragraph explaining why this interpretation would **not** be valid for a frequentist 90% confidence interval.

5. **Optional extension:** Apply the grid approximation to a political science scenario of your choice (a survey proportion, an election result, ...). Choose and justify a prior. Interpret the posterior.

::: notes
The homework is designed to progressively deepen the three takeaways from the session:
- Core 1 connects to "Bayes' theorem in full" -- students must explicitly compute P(data).
- Core 2 connects to "priors are assumptions" -- students must justify their choices in writing.
- Stretch 3 connects to "the posterior is a full distribution" -- sampling makes it tangible.
- Stretch 4 is the philosophically demanding question: the distinction between credible intervals and confidence intervals, stated in the student's own words.

The optional extension rewards students who want to get ahead and makes the ideas personally relevant by connecting to their own research interests.

Grade on a pass/fail basis as per syllabus. A submission that seriously engages with all Core and Stretch questions should pass. Empty or trivially incomplete submissions should not.
:::

---

## Thank you for your attention and see you next week!

<a href="https://github.com/dertristan" target="_blank" style="text-decoration: none; margin-left: 10px;"> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="20" height="20" fill="currentColor" class="bi bi-github" viewBox="0 0 16 16"> <path d="M8 0C3.58 0 0 3.58 0 8c0 3.54 2.29 6.53 5.47 7.59.4.07.55-.17.55-.38 0-.19-.01-.82-.01-1.49-2.01.37-2.53-.49-2.69-.94-.09-.23-.48-.94-.82-1.13-.28-.15-.68-.52-.01-.53.63-.01 1.08.58 1.23.82.72 1.21 1.87.87 2.33.66.07-.52.28-.87.51-1.07-1.78-.2-3.64-.89-3.64-3.95 0-.87.31-1.59.82-2.15-.08-.2-.36-1.02.08-2.12 0 0 .67-.21 2.2.82.64-.18 1.32-.27 2-.27s1.36.09 2 .27c1.53-1.04 2.2-.82 2.2-.82.44 1.1.16 1.92.08 2.12.51.56.82 1.27.82 2.15 0 3.07-1.87 3.75-3.65 3.95.29.25.54.73.54 1.48 0 1.07-.01 1.93-.01 2.2 0 .21.15.46.55.38A8.01 8.01 0 0 0 16 8c0-4.42-3.58-8-8-8"/> </svg> </a> <a href="mailto:tristan.muno@uni-mannheim.de" style="text-decoration: none; margin-left: 5px;"> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="20" height="20" fill="currentColor" class="bi bi-envelope" viewBox="0 0 16 16"> <path d="M0 4a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h12a2 2 0 0 1 2 2v8a2 2 0 0 1-2 2H2a2 2 0 0 1-2-2zm2-1a1 1 0 0 0-1 1v.217l7 4.2 7-4.2V4a1 1 0 0 0-1-1zm13 2.383-4.708 2.825L15 11.105zm-.034 6.876-5.64-3.471L8 9.583l-1.326-.795-5.64 3.47A1 1 0 0 0 2 13h12a1 1 0 0 0 .966-.741M1 11.105l4.708-2.897L1 5.383z"/> </svg> </a>
